Robot Experiment · Hardware Build

Dummy 13 — V2 Build Manual

Everything needed to build the simulation-trained walking robot for real: the parts and where to buy them, where every actuator goes, how it wires together, and how to put the trained brain on the chip. Every number on this page was measured in the verified simulation pipeline.

19servos (incl. 2 hip-yaw)
400 g · 32 cmV3 bracket construction
≈$130parts total (brackets are printed)
camera + mic + speakerit sees, hears and speaks
0 / 80falls under command + shoves (V3)
V3: buildable by construction (2026-07-15)
The V2 model failed its buildability audit — three servos per hip at the same coordinate, no leg part wide enough for an MG90S, STLs with no mounting provision. V3 fixes this the way real micro-humanoids are built: the servos ARE the structure. Each joint is a servo case plus a single-horn bracket; the links are printed side plates spanning between cases; every dimension is a real part dimension (an MG90S output shaft sits 5.9 mm off-centre, so every case in the model sits exactly where it would on the bench). The generator ends with an all-pairs overlap audit — zero intersecting parts — and assemblability is now a CI check, not an assumption. 32 cm, 400 g, 19 servos.
chain (each leg, top to bottom)part
pelvis rails2 printed rails + 2 crossbars; the hip-yaw servos hang beneath by their tabs
hip yaw → hip rollprinted U-plates from the yaw horn around the roll servo case
hip roll → hip pitchtwo-piece L-bracket from the roll horn to the pitch case
hip pitch → kneethigh side plates (58 mm), knee servo case across the shin top
knee → ankle pitchshin side plates (58 mm), ankle servo case across the shin bottom
ankle pitch → ankle roll → footoutboard plate, roll servo case, horn plate + 28×45 mm sole

00 What it can do

Every clip below is a real rollout of the actual trained policy in the verified simulator — nothing is scripted, sped up, or hand-animated, and the numbers are the measured evaluation rather than a target. This is the robot you are about to build.

Commanded forward velocity, on the buildable V3 body. Every speed tracks: 0.071 at 0.06, 0.096 at 0.09, 0.121 at 0.12, 0.139 at 0.14 m/s. Both feet swing 8–9 mm, symmetrically — clear of real-world floor clutter.

0.14 m/s commanded0.139 m/s achieved0 / 80 episodes falls

Spins on the spot at a commanded yaw rate (V3 body). This is what the two hip-yaw servos buy: without them the robot has no yaw joint at all and physically cannot turn.

−1.0 rad/s commanded−1.04 rad/s achieved0 / 80 episodes falls

Forward velocity and yaw rate together — a steered arc, which is how you actually drive it anywhere (V3 body, symmetric both directions).

0.10 m/s, ±0.6 rad/s commanded0.106/+0.62 · 0.105/−0.56 achieved0 / 80 falls

Zero command means stop, and stay stopped. A robot that cannot stop is not a robot you can drive — and this one holds still while being shoved (V3 body).

0 commanded0.012 m/s achievedrandom shoves under

Walks up a flight under its own steam (V2 sim body — being ported to V3, whose 8 mm foot clearance finally exceeds the riser height). It used to march in place at the bottom step forever; a riser-height curriculum taught it to commit.

4 mm × 6 steps riser16.6 mm (4 steps) climbed24 / 24 no-fall

Steers to a stream of goals across uneven ground, going AROUND obstacles it cannot climb (V2 sim body — port to V3 pending). Vision is the on-board camera.

4 mm bumps + clutter terrain1.8 goals/ep roamcamera + rays perception

Recovers from a collapse back to a held free stand (V2 sim body — port to V3 pending). Uptime is what separates a robot from a toy.

deep crouch / collapse from11 / 12 held free standautonomous recovery
Why it walks properly now
The gait used to limp: one foot cleared the ground by 10 mm and the other by 0.3 mm. That was not a training problem — at the original 8° hip-roll stand pose the feet sit 51 mm apart, so standing on one foot needs a 25 mm sideways shift of the centre of mass, and the roll joints can only produce about 12 mm. Single support was geometrically impossible, so no foot could ever fully unload. Narrowing the stance to about 2.5° fixed the limp — and with it the veer, because a one-sided gait walks in a circle. Build it with the narrow stance.

01 Print the frame

The Dummy 13 STLs are in this repo under stl/. Print at 100% scale (the robot stands ≈20 cm). Two additions to the stock frame, both discovered the hard way in training:

  • Back keel — a printed ridge running down the spine (see rear view, marker 2). Without it, a backwards fall settles into a turtle-shell rest the arms are too weak to escape. With it, falls roll to the side or front, which the robot can handle.
  • Front battery bay — the battery must sit on the torso front (front view, marker 2). Rear-mounted, the center of mass lands 12 mm behind the feet and the robot physically cannot stand.

02 Order the parts

PartRoleQtyPriceNotes
MG90S metal-gear micro servo10 leg joints10 (one 10-pack)$39.2913.6 g, 0.21 N·m — metal gears are mandatory for legs
SG90 micro servo7 arm + spine joints7≈$129 g, 0.12 N·m — plastic gears fine for arms
Seeed XIAO ESP32S3 Sensebrain + camera + MIC + WiFi1$13.998 MB PSRAM; runs both neural nets on-board. The microphone is already on this board (PDM MEMS, GPIO42 clk / GPIO41 data, 16 kHz mono) — the robot's ears cost nothing extra
MG90S servo — hip yawlets the robot TURN2≈$8Do not skip. Without a yaw joint the robot has no way to rotate about its vertical axis at all — it can walk, but it cannot be steered (measured: commanded 1.0 rad/s → achieved −0.02)
MAX98357A I2S amplifiergives the robot a VOICE1≈$4BCLK→SCK, LRC→LRCK, DIN→SD on the XIAO; 5 V. Add 100 µF + 0.1 µF across VCC–GND
8 Ω 1 W speaker (20–28 mm)voice output1≈$25 g — chest front, firing forward beside the camera
OV2640 wide-angle lens (120°)replaces the stock ~66° lens1≈$4MANDATORY — the stock lens cannot see the ±60° obstacle fan
PCA9685 16-ch PWM driverservo control over I2C2≈$12solder A0 on the second board → address 0x41
CD74HC4067 16-ch analog muxreads all 17 servo pots2≈$9the $3 mod that gives the robot true joint sensing
MPU-6050 IMU (GY-521)orientation + angular velocity1≈$5shares the I2C bus with the PCA9685s
2S 500 mAh LiPopower1≈$1240 g — mount FRONT of torso
5 V 3 A UBECservo + logic rail1≈$9never feed servos from the XIAO's regulator
Printed bracket + plate set (V3)the body IS the servos + these1 print runfilament≈55 g of PETG: pelvis rails, hip U-plates, L-brackets, thigh/shin side plates, ankle plates, soles. Generated from the audited model (mujoco/_make_v3.py)
Dummy 13 shells (optional)cosmetic covers onlyas desiredfilamentthe original figure STLs — NOT structural; V2's attempt to use them as the frame failed its buildability audit
Two purchases people skip, and shouldn't:
The 120° wide-angle lens — the obstacle sensing was trained on a ±60° fan, and the stock ~66° lens physically cannot see it (measured: perception collapses).
The two analog muxes — soldering one wire to each servo's potentiometer wiper and reading it through a mux gives true joint positions. With them the navigation policy scores 83%; with dead-reckoning from commands alone it scores 0%. It is a ~$9, one-evening mod and it is the difference between a robot and a puppet.

03 Place the actuators

Marker positions below are projected from the simulation model itself — they are exact. Legs use the metal-gear MG90S (10), arms and spine the lighter SG90 (7). Left and right sides mirror each other.

Each servo maps to one PWM channel. Set every servo to its stand angle before pressing on the horn (step 07) — the trained brain assumes this zero.

ChJointServoStandRangeDriver
0hip_pitch_lMG90S+4.0°−55…+65°PCA 0x40
1hip_roll_lMG90S+8.0°−35…+35°PCA 0x40
2knee_lMG90S−12.0°−120…0°PCA 0x40
3ankle_pitch_lMG90S+10.0°−45…+25°PCA 0x40
4ankle_roll_lMG90S0.0°−20…+20°PCA 0x40
5hip_pitch_rMG90S+4.0°−55…+65°PCA 0x40
6hip_roll_rMG90S−8.0°−35…+35°PCA 0x40
7knee_rMG90S−12.0°−120…0°PCA 0x40
8ankle_pitch_rMG90S+10.0°−45…+25°PCA 0x40
9ankle_roll_rMG90S0.0°−20…+20°PCA 0x40
10spine_pitchSG900.0°−15…+40°PCA 0x41 ch 0
11shoulder_pitch_lSG900.0°−65…+55°PCA 0x41 ch 1
12shoulder_roll_lSG900.0°−35…+35°PCA 0x41 ch 2
13elbow_lSG90+30.0°0…+120°PCA 0x41 ch 3
14shoulder_pitch_rSG900.0°−65…+55°PCA 0x41 ch 4
15shoulder_roll_rSG900.0°−35…+35°PCA 0x41 ch 5
16elbow_rSG90+30.0°0…+120°PCA 0x41 ch 6

04 Mount the electronics

  • XIAO ESP32S3 Sense — chest front, camera lens facing forward and tilted ~15° down. This is the entire brain: it runs the vision net and the walking policy.
  • 2× PCA9685 — stacked on the upper back. Board 1 stays at address 0x40 (legs, channels 0–9); solder jumper A0 on board 2 → 0x41 (arms + spine).
  • MPU-6050 — anywhere rigid on the torso, axes aligned with the body.
  • UBEC — upper back beside the PCA stack; battery leads route over the shoulder.

05 Wire it

POWER                                 SIGNAL (I2C bus, 3V3)
2S LiPo (7.4 V)                       XIAO SDA (GPIO5) ──┬── PCA9685 #1 (0x40)
   │                                  XIAO SCL (GPIO6) ──┼── PCA9685 #2 (0x41)
   └─► UBEC 5 V 3 A                                      └── MPU-6050 (0x68)
         ├─► PCA9685 V+ rails (all 17 servos)
         └─► XIAO 5V pin              POT TAPS (true joint angles)
                                      each servo pot wiper ──► CD74HC4067 C0..C15
COMMON GROUND: battery, UBEC,         mux1 SIG + mux2 SIG ──► GPIO2 (ADC)
XIAO, both PCAs, both muxes, IMU      S0–S3 select ──► GPIO3,4,7,8 (shared)
                                      mux2 EN ──► GPIO9 (inverted select)
The pot-tap mod: open each servo, solder one thin wire to the potentiometer's middle pin, close it up, route the wire with the servo lead. 17 wires → two muxes → one ADC pin. Sample all 17 in a fast loop (≥500 Hz) and derive joint velocity by finite difference — the firmware constant for this is already in the exported header.

06 Flash the brain

The trained networks are already exported as portable C in firmware/ — no Python, no frameworks on the robot:

  • dummy13_policy.h / dummy13_infer.c — the navigation policy (82 → 256 → 256 → 17). int8 weights: 91 KB, ~2–3 ms per tick at 240 MHz. Matches the trained model to 10⁻⁶.
  • cam_student.h / cam_infer.c — the camera → obstacle-distance net (96×96 input). ~12 M MACs ≈ 2–3 Hz vision; the 50 Hz control loop reuses the latest estimate between frames.
  • The 50 Hz control loop skeleton, the observation layout, and the servo post-processing (EMA smoothing → slew limit → clip) are documented in firmware/README.md — the sim and the firmware share these constants exactly; do not retune them.

07 Calibrate

  1. Power servos before attaching horns. Command every channel to its stand angle from the table above (MG90S/SG90: 500–2500 µs ≈ ±90°).
  2. Press each horn on so the printed limb sits in the stand pose: knees −12°, ankles +10°, hips +4°, hip roll splayed ±8°, elbows +30°.
  3. Record each pot's ADC reading at −45°, 0°, +45° → per-joint linear map (the pot taps are uncalibrated voltage dividers; three points per joint is enough).

08 First boot — in this order

  1. Stand (zero-action): the stand pose is a stable equilibrium. If it topples, re-check horn alignment and battery placement before anything else.
  2. Walk: flash the pot-hardened walker. Sim-verified: 1.38 m / 12 s, 0 falls / 8 under exactly this sensing.
  3. Recover: crouch it by hand, let go — the get-up policy scores 11/12 under pot sensing.
  4. Navigate: scatter objects, give it a goal. The honest sim numbers under the full hardware condition (camera + pots + int8): 75% crossing success; long multi-goal missions compound per-leg risk (~40% over five commands) — expect that, it is arithmetic, not a fault.
What is verified vs. not: every number above comes from the simulation configured to this exact hardware (masses, servo model, sensing path, quantized weights). What sim cannot promise: servo heat, lens focus, floor texture. Expect a gap; measure it; the training pipeline can absorb what you measure.
Dummy 13 V2 · generated from the ROBOT_EXPERIMENT simulation pipeline · placement markers are projected from the physics model, not drawn by hand. Regenerate after structural changes: mujoco/_render_buildviews.py → build-guide/_make_site.py.
